Section 1

Project Overview

Business Context

SpaceX has disrupted the commercial space industry by dramatically reducing launch costs through rocket reusability. The Falcon 9 first stage booster — worth approximately $62 million per mission — can be recovered, refurbished, and re-flown, enabling SpaceX to offer launches at roughly $67 million compared to competitors charging over $165 million.

Falcon 9 Vehicle Evolution — v1.0 through Block 5, Falcon Heavy, and Dragon capsule

Predicting whether the first stage will successfully land is critical for cost estimation, mission planning, and competitive intelligence. This project builds machine learning models to predict Falcon 9 first stage landing outcomes using publicly available data.

Project Objectives

  • Collect Falcon 9 launch data from the SpaceX REST API and Wikipedia via web scraping
  • Clean, wrangle, and engineer features from raw launch records
  • Perform exploratory data analysis using matplotlib, seaborn, SQL, Folium, and Plotly Dash
  • Build and tune classification models to predict first stage landing success
  • Identify the best-performing algorithm and key predictive features

Dataset Description

The dataset contains 90 Falcon 9 launches with the following key features:

  • FlightNumber — sequential launch identifier
  • BoosterVersion — version of the Falcon 9 booster (v1.0, v1.1, FT, B4, B5)
  • PayloadMass — payload mass in kilograms
  • Orbit — target orbit type (LEO, GTO, ISS, SSO, etc.)
  • LaunchSite — CCAFS SLC 40, KSC LC 39A, VAFB SLC 4E
  • Outcome — booster landing result
  • class — binary target: 1 = successful landing, 0 = failed

Methodology: 5 Phases

  1. Phase 1A: Data Collection via SpaceX REST API
  2. Phase 1B: Data Collection via Web Scraping (Wikipedia)
  3. Phase 2: Data Wrangling and Feature Engineering
  4. Phase 3: Exploratory Data Analysis (Visualization + SQL)
  5. Phase 4: Interactive Visual Analytics (Folium + Plotly Dash)
  6. Phase 5: Predictive Analysis — Classification Models
Phase 1A

Data Collection: SpaceX REST API

Orbit Type Reference

Understanding orbit types is essential for interpreting launch outcomes. Each orbit places different demands on the vehicle's fuel budget, directly affecting booster recovery feasibility.

LEO — Low Earth Orbit

Altitude ≤ 2,000 km. The most commonly used orbit — host to the ISS, Starlink, and Earth observation constellations. Relatively low energy allows for straightforward booster recovery.

VLEO — Very Low Earth Orbit

Altitude < 450 km. Benefits Earth observation due to proximity but requires frequent re-boosting. Higher atmospheric drag at this altitude creates challenges for sustained operations.

GTO — Geostationary Transfer Orbit

High-energy orbit used to reach GEO (~35,786 km). Heavy payloads and high velocity make first-stage recovery extremely challenging — most GTO missions do not attempt landing.

GEO — Geostationary Orbit

Circular orbit at exactly 35,786 km — satellite appears stationary over Earth. Used for weather, comms, and surveillance. Highest energy mission profile.

SSO — Sun-Synchronous Orbit

Nearly polar orbit where the satellite passes over any point at the same local solar time. Ideal for consistent Earth imaging. Typically associated with high landing success.

MEO — Medium Earth Orbit

2,000 – 35,786 km altitude. Used for GPS and navigation constellations. Orbital period ~12 hours.

HEO — Highly Elliptical Orbit

High eccentricity orbit around Earth. Provides long dwell time over high latitudes. Used for communications in polar regions and scientific missions.

ISS — International Space Station

Modular LEO station at ~408 km. Multinational collaboration (NASA, Roscosmos, JAXA, ESA, CSA). Resupply missions under CRS program are a key SpaceX revenue source.

ES-L1 — Earth–Sun Lagrange Point 1

Gravitational equilibrium point between Earth and Sun (~1.5M km from Earth). Used for solar observation missions such as DSCOVR. Very high energy — no booster recovery.

PO — Polar Orbit

Passes above or near both poles. Allows imaging of the entire Earth surface over successive passes. Used for reconnaissance, weather, and Earth science.

Orbit altitude comparison — LEO (1,000 km), MEO (10,000 km), HEO (elliptical), GEO (35,786 km)

Libraries & Helper Functions

The data collection pipeline was built using requests for HTTP calls and pandas for data structuring. Four helper functions were defined to decode the opaque ID references returned by the API into human-readable values:

  • getBoosterVersion() — Calls /rockets/{id} to resolve each rocket ID into a booster version name (e.g. Falcon 9 v1.1, FT, B5).
  • getLaunchSite() — Calls /launchpads/{id} to retrieve the full site name, longitude, and latitude for each launch location.
  • getPayloadData() — Calls /payloads/{id} to retrieve payload mass (kg) and target orbit type for each mission.
  • getCoreData() — Calls /cores/{id} to retrieve landing outcome, landing type (ASDS drone ship / RTLS), number of prior flights, and grid fin status.

API Request & Response

A GET request was made to the SpaceX v4 REST API endpoint GET /launches/past, returning a JSON array of all past launches. A static snapshot hosted on IBM Cloud Object Storage was used to ensure reproducibility across sessions. The response returned a 200 OK status and was parsed using pd.json_normalize() — flattening the nested JSON structure into a tabular DataFrame.

The raw response contained heavily nested ID references — every rocket, launchpad, payload, and core was represented only by a UUID string, not by human-readable values. The four helper functions above were applied to resolve each ID through secondary API calls, enriching the dataset with meaningful labels.

Building the Launch DataFrame

After all helper functions were applied, the resolved values were assembled into a structured Python dictionary — launch_dict — with the following columns:

FlightNumber Date BoosterVersion PayloadMass Orbit LaunchSite Outcome Flights GridFins Reused Legs LandingPad Block ReusedCount Serial Longitude Latitude

This dictionary was converted into a pandas DataFrame — the clean, enriched dataset ready for filtering and wrangling.

Filtering to Falcon 9 Only

The raw dataset included launches from the earlier Falcon 1 vehicle, which is structurally different from the Falcon 9 and irrelevant to the landing prediction objective. These records were filtered out, retaining only rows where BoosterVersion != 'Falcon 1'. The FlightNumber column was then reset sequentially from 1 to reflect the correct Falcon 9 launch count.

The resulting dataset contains 90 Falcon 9 launches — the core dataset used for all subsequent analysis, visualisation, and machine learning phases.

Phase 1B

Data Collection: Web Scraping

Approach

The Wikipedia page listing all Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy launches was scraped using BeautifulSoup and requests. The HTML table was parsed row by row to extract flight numbers, dates, booster versions, launch sites, payloads, orbits, customers, launch outcomes, and booster landing results — producing a structured DataFrame ready for wrangling.

A set of helper functions handled edge cases in the table markup — stripping superscript references, normalising Unicode characters in payload mass strings, and extracting multi-line cell content. The parsed records were assembled into a pandas DataFrame and exported as spacex_web_scraped.csv.

Phase 3A

Exploratory Data Analysis: Visualization

Approach

Used matplotlib and seaborn to visualize relationships between launch features and landing outcomes. Charts explore how flight number, payload mass, orbit type, launch site, and year correlate with success rates. Color coding distinguishes successful landings (Class 1) from failures (Class 0).

Flight Number vs Payload Mass — colored by landing outcome (Class 0 = fail, 1 = success)

Flight Number vs Launch Site — success/failure distribution across sites

Payload Mass vs Launch Site — heavy payload missions per site

Orbit Type Success Rate — mean landing class by orbit

Flight Number vs Orbit Type — colored by landing outcome

Payload Mass vs Orbit Type — landing success distribution

Annual Landing Success Rate (2010–2020) — trend shows dramatic improvement from 2015 onward

Phase 3B

Exploratory Data Analysis: SQL

Approach

Loaded the SpaceX dataset into an IBM Db2 cloud database and executed 10 SQL queries using the ipython-sql extension and SQLAlchemy. Queries explored unique launch sites, payload totals, mission outcomes, booster versions, and landing results by date range.

Key SQL Findings

  • Query 1 — Unique launch sites: CCAFS LC-40, CCAFS SLC-40, KSC LC-39A, VAFB SLC-4E
  • Query 2 — CCA-prefixed sites: CCAFS LC-40 is the dominant early-program site
  • Query 3 — NASA CRS payload total: 45,596 kg delivered to ISS across CRS missions
  • Query 4 — F9 v1.1 average payload: 2,928 kg average payload mass
  • Query 5 — First ground pad landing: 2015-12-22 (historic OG-2 mission)
  • Query 6 — Drone ship landings (4,000–6,000 kg): F9 FT B1022, B1026, B1021.2, B1031.2
  • Query 7 — Mission outcomes: 99 Successes, 1 In-flight failure, 1 unclear
  • Query 8 — Max payload boosters: B5 series (B1048, B1049, B1051, B1058, B1060) carry heaviest payloads
  • Query 9 — 2015 drone ship failures: F9 v1.1 B1012 and B1015, both from CCAFS LC-40
  • Query 10 — Landing outcomes (2010–Mar 2017): 10 no-attempt, 5 drone ship failures, 5 drone ship successes, 3 ground pad successes
Phase 4

Interactive Visual Analytics: Folium and Plotly Dash

Folium Interactive Maps

Used Folium to create interactive maps showing the geographic distribution of SpaceX launch sites. Launch outcomes were color-coded (green = success, red = failure) with marker clusters for density visualization. Distance calculations from each site to the nearest coastline, highway, and railway were performed.

Folium map — SpaceX launch sites with success/failure markers and proximity analysis

Launch site geographic analysis — coastline, highway, and railway distance overlays

Plotly Dash Interactive Dashboard

A Plotly Dash web application was built with two interactive components:

  • Pie Chart: Overall launch success proportion with site filter dropdown
  • Scatter Plot: Payload Mass vs. Landing Outcome by booster version with payload range slider

The dashboard allows filtering by individual launch site or all sites combined, and by payload mass range (0–10,000 kg).

Plotly Dash — pie chart showing overall launch success rate across all sites

Plotly Dash — success rate filtered by individual launch site

Plotly Dash — payload mass vs landing outcome scatter plot

Plotly Dash — filtered by payload mass range with booster version color coding

Key Geospatial Findings

  • All SpaceX launch sites are located within 50 km of the coastline — essential for ocean trajectory and booster landing safety
  • KSC LC 39A (Kennedy Space Center) shows the highest success rate and is the primary site for heavier payloads
  • VAFB SLC 4E in California handles polar orbit missions (SSO), which show high landing success rates
  • CCAFS SLC 40 has the largest total number of launches, accumulating the most mission history
  • All sites are in close proximity to railways and highways — enabling rapid booster recovery and transport logistics
Phase 5

Predictive Analysis: Classification Models

Approach

Four classification algorithms were trained and evaluated using GridSearchCV for hyperparameter tuning with 10-fold cross-validation. The dataset was split 80/20 for training and testing. Features were standardized using StandardScaler. One-hot encoding was applied to categorical features (LaunchSite, Orbit, BoosterVersion).

Confusion Matrices

Confusion matrices show true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each model on the test set.

Logistic Regression — confusion matrix on test set (83.3% accuracy)

Support Vector Machine — confusion matrix on test set (83.3% accuracy)

Decision Tree — confusion matrix on test set (94.4% accuracy)

K-Nearest Neighbors — confusion matrix on test set (88.9% accuracy)

Cross-validation best scores by algorithm — Decision Tree achieves highest CV accuracy (90.4%)

Test set accuracy scores by algorithm — Decision Tree leads with 94.4%

Model Performance Summary

Algorithm Best CV Accuracy Test Accuracy Best Parameters Status
Logistic Regression 84.6% 83.3% C=0.01, penalty=l2, solver=lbfgs Baseline
Support Vector Machine 84.8% 83.3% C=1.0, gamma=0.032, kernel=sigmoid Comparable
Decision Tree 90.4% 94.4% criterion=entropy, max_depth=6, max_features=sqrt BEST MODEL
K-Nearest Neighbors 87.7% 88.9% algorithm=auto, n_neighbors=10, p=1 Strong
Section 10

Key Findings and Conclusion

Data & Feature Findings

Success rate trend: SpaceX's Falcon 9 landing success rate improved dramatically over time, from near 0% before 2015 to consistently above 80% by 2019–2020, reflecting rapid technological iteration.
Launch site performance: KSC LC 39A shows the highest success rate. CCAFS SLC 40 has the most launches but a lower rate due to early experimental missions.
Orbit type impact: SSO, ES-L1, and HEO orbits show 100% landing success. GTO orbits (geostationary transfer) show lower success rates, likely due to heavier payloads consuming fuel reserves needed for landing burns.
Payload mass threshold: Heavier payloads (above approximately 6,000 kg) correlate with lower landing success — higher mass leaves less fuel for landing burns.
Booster version improvement: Falcon 9 FT and later Block 5 variants show significantly higher success rates than v1.0 and v1.1, indicating design maturity.
Overall success rate: 60 out of 90 launches (66.7%) resulted in successful first stage recovery.

Geospatial Findings

Coastline proximity: All launch sites are within 1 km of the Atlantic or Pacific coastline — enabling ocean trajectories and booster recovery at sea landing zones.
Infrastructure: Every site is within 20 km of major highways and railways, enabling efficient booster transport back to refurbishment facilities.
Equatorial proximity: Florida sites (CCAFS, KSC) are at ~28 degrees latitude, providing Earth's rotational boost for eastward launches and geostationary missions.

Machine Learning Findings

Best model: Decision Tree with entropy criterion and max_depth=6 achieved the highest test accuracy at 94.4%, outperforming Logistic Regression and SVM by over 11 percentage points.
Generalization: Decision Tree's test accuracy (94.4%) exceeds its CV accuracy (90.4%), suggesting the test split was favorable — but the model generalizes well overall.
SVM vs. Logistic Regression: Both achieved identical test accuracy (83.3%), showing they capture similar linear boundaries in feature space.
KNN performance: KNN achieved 88.9% test accuracy — second best — benefiting from the non-parametric, proximity-based classification approach.
Key predictive features: FlightNumber (experience), PayloadMass, Orbit type (one-hot encoded), and LaunchSite are the most informative features for landing prediction.

Conclusion

This capstone project demonstrates an end-to-end data science pipeline — from raw API data collection through exploratory analysis, interactive visualization, and predictive modeling — applied to the real-world problem of SpaceX Falcon 9 landing prediction.

The Decision Tree classifier with tuned hyperparameters achieves 94.4% accuracy on the test set, making it a highly reliable predictor of first stage landing success. This prediction capability has direct business value: competitors and commercial customers can use such a model to estimate whether a competing SpaceX launch will reduce costs through booster recovery, informing their own pricing strategies.

The analysis confirms that SpaceX's success rate has dramatically improved over time due to technological iteration, and that mission parameters (orbit type, payload mass, launch site, and booster version) are strong predictors of recovery success.
